description:
You are acting as a Site-Reliability Engineer who has just inherited a very old
  monitoring utility.  
  Everything you need is already on–disk inside  
  /home/user/legacy_tools/ :

    • /home/user/legacy_tools/hosts.lst  
      – newline-separated list of three hosts (kept in exactly this order).  

    • /home/user/legacy_tools/run_checks.sh  
      – a vintage shell script that takes a single host name as its only
        argument and writes one short line describing that host’s reachability.
        Although you may inspect the script, **do not modify it**; treat it as
        read-only production code.

  Your job is to produce a brand-new consolidated report,
  /home/user/reports/uptime_summary.csv, by orchestrating calls to the legacy
  script.

  Required report format (this is what the automated grading system will read):

    1. File path: /home/user/reports/uptime_summary.csv  
       – the enclosing directory must already exist or be created by you.  

    2. Encoding & line endings: UTF-8, UNIX “\n”.  

    3. Pure CSV with exactly four columns in this precise order:
         timestamp,host,status,latency_ms

       • Header row must be the very first line and must match the column names
         above **byte-for-byte**.  

       • Every subsequent line corresponds to **one** host in the exact order
         they appear in hosts.lst.  
         Example skeleton (your actual timestamp value will differ):  

           timestamp,host,status,latency_ms
           2024-03-26T14:05:02Z,127.0.0.1,UP,1
           2024-03-26T14:05:02Z,localhost,UP,2
           2024-03-26T14:05:02Z,blackhole.local,DOWN,

    4. Field-level rules:

       • timestamp – ISO-8601 in UTC, truncated to seconds, e.g.
         2024-03-26T14:05:02Z.  
         Use the **same** timestamp for every data row produced in one run.  

       • status – literal strings “UP” or “DOWN” exactly as produced by
         run_checks.sh; propagate them without re-spelling.  

       • latency_ms – an **integer** round-trip value when status is “UP”.
         If status equals “DOWN”, leave the field completely empty (i.e.,
         two consecutive commas at the end of the line).  

  Steps you are expected to perform (high-level):

    1. Read the host list from hosts.lst (maintain order).  
    2. Invoke /home/user/legacy_tools/run_checks.sh once per host.  
       – Parse the output to discover status and (when present) latency.  
       – The script’s exit code is also meaningful (0 = UP, non-zero = DOWN).  
    3. Compose the CSV exactly as specified.  
    4. Save the file at the expected path and then print its full contents to
       STDOUT so a human operator (and the automated grader) can see the final
       result.

  NOTE:
    • Do not attempt to rewrite run_checks.sh; consider it production code.  
    • The grading system will check the CSV’s existence, order, header,
      timestamp format, status spelling, empty latency for DOWN hosts, and
      integer latency for UP hosts.  
    • Any additional commentary you wish to show must be sent to STDERR or
      appear *before/after* the CSV content so the file itself stays pristine.

  Your terminal session should finish with the correctly formatted
  /home/user/reports/uptime_summary.csv stored on disk **and** echoed to the
  console.
truth:
<!--
    Ground-truth data used by the automated test harness.
    DO NOT reveal any of this in the public task description.
  -->

  <preexisting>
    <!-- Files already present when the task starts -->
    <file path="/home/user/legacy_tools/hosts.lst" mode="0644"><![CDATA[
127.0.0.1
localhost
blackhole.local
]]></file>

    <file path="/home/user/legacy_tools/run_checks.sh" mode="0755"><![CDATA[
#!/usr/bin/env bash
#
# Vintage reachability probe used by ops long ago.
# Usage: ./run_checks.sh <host>
# It prints exactly one line and returns a meaningful exit code.
HOST="$1"

case "$HOST" in
  "127.0.0.1")
        echo "STATUS:UP LATENCY_MS:1"
        exit 0
        ;;
  "localhost")
        echo "STATUS:UP LATENCY_MS:2"
        exit 0
        ;;
  *)
        echo "STATUS:DOWN"
        exit 2
        ;;
esac
]]></file>
  </preexisting>

  <expected_output>
    <!--
      The grader will allow *any* valid ISO-8601 timestamp generated at runtime.
      It will verify:
        * header row matches exactly.
        * three data rows exist and maintain host order.
        * status words and latency numbers match the deterministic behaviour
          of run_checks.sh.
    -->
    <csv_path>/home/user/reports/uptime_summary.csv</csv_path>

    <csv_regex>
      ^timestamp,host,status,latency_ms\n # exact header
      [0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}Z,127\.0\.0\.1,UP,1\n
      \1,localhost,UP,2\n
      \1,blackhole\.local,DOWN,\n$
    </csv_regex>
    <!-- Explanation: \1 back-reference ties the identical timestamp across rows -->
  </expected_output>
name: task_000000_08164c15
